text:
Admission Date:  1974-01-25              Discharge Date:   1981-07-07

Date of Birth:  1987-06-07             Sex:   F

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Alison
Chief Complaint:
Post-op Hypoxia

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Percutaneous fixation of Right acetabular column fracture

History of Present Illness:
81 F with PMH of AF on coumadin, HTN, HLD, hypothyroidism,
anxiety, depression, and IBS admitted to the ortho service with
a R acetabular fracture after a mechanical fall yesterday. She
was taken to the OR today for an ORIF. Intraoperatively, she had
bronchospasm that resolved with albuterol. Post-operatively,
when she was extaubted she had diffuse wheezing and was hypoxic
to 80's on nasal cannula. Her O2 sat increased to 93-95% on an
NRB. She was transitioned to BIPAP with increase in sats 95-97%.
CXR showed pulmonary edema. She received lasix 10mg IV, then
20mg IV with 1600cc of urine output. She is being transferred to
the ICU for pulmonary edema requiring BIPAP.

Past Medical History:
PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
Atrial Fibrillation (CHADS2 is 2)
Hypertension
Hyperlipidemia
Hypothyroidism
Anxiety
Depression
IBS
S/p Appendectomy


Social History:
SOCIAL HISTORY: From 74534 Reynolds Terrace Apt. 499
Gilbertmouth, FM 87426-8565, retired, no tobacco, rare EtOH, no
drugs. Married. Husband is retired internist.

Family History:
Non-contributory

Physical Exam:
On evaulation in the PACU:

Vitals: T: afebrile BP:126/76 P:65 R: 18 O2:95%
General: Alert, oriented, no acute distress. Breathing
comfortably on NRB.
HEENT: Sclera anicteric.
Neck: supple, JVP ~10cm, no LAD
Lungs: Diffuse wheezing anteriorly with scattered rhonchi.
CV: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs,
gallops
Abdomen: soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds present,
no rebound tenderness or guarding, no organomegaly
GU: foley draining clear yellow urine.
Ext: surgical dressings on Right leg. Left leg and Right LE
distal to bandaging were warm, well perfused, 2+ pulses, no
clubbing, cyanosis or edema

On discharge:
Vitals: T: 98.8, BP: 116/61, P: 81, RR: 18, O2: 95% on RA
General: Pt comfortable, in NAD
Lungs: fine crackles at the bases bilaterally, no wheezes
CV: RRR, S1, S2, no murmurs/rubs/gallops
Ext: Right leg externally rotated, no clubbing/cyanosis/edema

CT Pelvis:
1. Comminuted right acetabular fracture with extension into the
right iliac
Jonathan and inferior pubic ramus with adjacent hematoma. No
evidence of active
extravasation. Note that traumatic bladder injury is not
excluded and if this
is a concern clinically, would recommend further evaluation with
a CT
cystogram.
2. Atherosclerotic disease with abdominal aortic ectasia.
3. Diverticulosis.
.
CT C-Spine:
IMPRESSION: No acute fracture or traumatic malalignment. Note
that
degenerative changes with osteophyte formation slightly efface
the ventral
thecal sac, which may predispose to traumatic cord injury. If
the physical
examination of the patient is concerning for this entity, this
could be
further evaluated with MRI, if no contraindication.
.
The study and the report were reviewed by the staff radiologist
.
CT Cystogram:
.
IMPRESSION:
.
1. No contrast extravasation to suggest urinary bladder rupture.

2. Extensive right acetabular fractures with adjacent hematoma,
unchanged
from the recent comparison studies.
.
02-06 CXR:
REASON FOR EXAM: Desaturation, assess for aspiration or foreign
body.
.
Comparison is made with prior study 09-03.
 .
Mild-to-moderate cardiomegaly is stable. New diffuse lung
opacities are
larger in the right upper lobe and left lower lobe. The
differential
diagnosis is broad and include massive aspiration, hemorrhage. I
doubt, this
corresponds to asymmetric pulmonary edema or contusions. There
is no
pneumothorax or large pleural effusions
.
08-17 CXR AP/LAT:
IMPRESSION: Interval improvement in pulmonary vascular
congestion and
probable focal infiltrate at the left lung base.
.
ECHO 1993-11-19:
The left atrium is elongated. There is mild symmetric left
ventricular hypertrophy with normal cavity size and
regional/global systolic function (LVEF>55%). The right
ventricular cavity is moderately dilated with borderline normal
free wall function. The diameters of aorta at the sinus,
ascending and arch levels are normal. The aortic valve leaflets
(3) are mildly thickened. There is no valvular aortic stenosis.
The increased transaortic velocity is likely related to
increased stroke volume due to aortic regurgitation. Mild (1+)
aortic regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are
mildly thickened. There is no mitral valve prolapse. Trivial
mitral regurgitation is seen. The tricuspid valve leaflets are
mildly thickened. Moderate [2+] tricuspid regurgitation is seen.
There is moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. There
is a trivial/physiologic pericardial effusion.
.
IMPRESSION: Mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with
normal systolic function. Dilated right ventricle with moderate
tricuspid regurgitation and moderate pulmonary artery systolic
hypertension.
.
09-27 HIP X-RAY:
IMPRESSION: Right acetabular fracture, status post ORIF as
above. Please see recent CT for fracture details


Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. Patterson is an 81-year-old woman with afib on coumadin,
HTN, HLD, IBS and had a mechanical fall on 2002-04-21, resulting
in a Right acetabular column fracture. The patient underwent an
ORIF of her fracture. And had post operative difficulty
ventilating in the PACU. She was transferred to the the ICU and
was treated with BIPAP.
.
1. Pulmonary Edema: Post-operatively, when she was extaubted she
had diffuse wheezing and was hypoxic to 80's on nasal cannula.
Her O2 sat increased to 93-95% on an NRB. She was transitioned
to BIPAP with increase in sats 95-97%. CXR showed pulmonary
edema. She received lasix 10mg IV, then 20mg IV with 1600 cc of
urine output. She was weaned to 4 L of nasal canula.  She was
transferred to the ICU for pulmonary edema requiring BIPAP.
.
In the ICU, the patient was further diuresed 3 boluses of 20 mg
Lasix IV and put out an additional 2 L urine.  After, she put
out 2 L of additional urine and was weaned to 3 L NC and was
saturating at 93%.  She was called out to the floor.
.
On the floor, the patient continued to tolerate weaning and was
eventually weaned off oxygen and saturated 95% on RA.  She
received one additional dose of lasix on 08-17 (40 mg PO) and
continued to improve.  She had a chest x-ray showing marked
improvement of her pulmonary edema. At the time of discharge she
was saturating 94% on room air.
.
2. Pneumonia: In the ICU, the patient was found to have a left
lower lobe pneumonia.  She was initially started on zosyn in the
ICU. On the floor she was changed to levofloxacin and zosyn to
cover community acquired pneumonia and aspiration pneumonia.
The patient had been in the hospital for less than 48 hours, so
she did not need to be treated for hospital acquired pneumonia.
The patient will continue on levofloxacin and flagyl for the
full course and finish the medications on 1985-10-25. She was
afebrile for the five days prior to discharge.
.
3. Acetabular Fracture: Patient sustained mechanical fall at
home and sustained an acetabular fracture. She underwent
percutaneous fixation of Right acetabular column fracture on
1974-01-25.  The patient should have her stitches removed 2 weeks
following surgery. She has an appointment scheduled with
orthopedics to remove these.
.
5. Atrial Fibrillation: Stable on sotolol. Coumadin was
initially held for surgery, but then was restarted on 05-18.  She
reached a therapeutic INR on the day of discharge. The patient's
sotolol was continued.
.
6. Hyptertension: Continued on home meds of hydrochlorothiazide,
isosorbide dinitrate, and olmesartan with good control.
.
7. Depression: Continued on lexapro, Klonipin qHS
.
8. Hypothyroidism: Continued on levothyroxine


Medications on Admission:

Olmesartan 40 mg Oral daily

Vitamin D 800 UNIT PO/NG DAILY

Coumadin 4 mg PO daily

Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Kings Daughters Medical Center Ohio Healthcare, NH

Discharge Diagnosis:
PRIMARY:
acetabular fracture
pulmonary edema
pneumonia

SECONDARY:
hypertension
atrial fibrillation


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker
or cane). Partial weight bearing on right.


Discharge Instructions:
It was a pleasure to participate in your care Ms. Patterson.
You were admitted to the hospital for a fracture of your right
hip and had surgery.  Following surgery, you had difficulty
breathing because you had too much fluid in your lungs.  You
were transferred to the intensive care unit to use a machine
that could help you to breathe.  You also developed a pneumonia
and we treated you with antibiotics.  Your breathing improved
when we gave you medicine to remove fluid from your lungs.  You
were transferred from the ICU to the floor and were able to stop
using the oxygen.  Physical therapy worked with you and
recommended that you go to a rehab facility.

We made the following changes to your medications:
1. Added lovenox - your rehab will continue this for the next
03-08
2. Added calcium
3. Added vitamin D
4. Added Acetaminophen-Codeine 300-30 mg Tablet PO Q6H (every 6
hours) as needed for pain.

Followup Instructions:
Follow-up appointment with Dr. Diana Turner Wednesday 12-13 at 11:20am on 988 Michael Isle
West Joannabury, SC 60581-7834 Washington Regional Medical Center ICU Center. Please arrive
at 11am for X-rays before your visit.

Additionally, please follow up with the doctor at the rehab
facility. As well, please call your primary doctor for follow-up
within one week of leaving the facility.
